coredata.dc:title: CuO modified ZnO on nitrogen-doped carbon: a durable and efficient electrocatalyst for oxygen reduction reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Mahato, D.
Gurusamy, T.
Jain, S.K.
Ramanujam, K.
Haridoss, P.
Thomas, T.
coredata.dc:description: The sluggish nature of oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and the current use of expensive precious metals (platinum [Pt], palladium [Pd], etc.) are barriers to the commercialization and scale-up of fuel cell and metal-air battery technologies. Given this, the development of low-cost non-precious metal-based electrocatalysts with high activity and durability is an area that requires attention. In this study, we report cupric oxide (CuO) modified zinc oxide (ZnO) supported on nitrogen-doped carbon (CuO/ZnO/NC-600) as a desirable electrocatalyst given its activity in alkaline medium, durability, and low cost. The CuO/ZnO/NC-600 catalyst shows excellent ORR activity with an onset potential and half-wave potential (E 1/2) of 0.91 V and 0.80 V vs. reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE), respectively, with outstanding limiting current density of 5.34 mA/cm2. The catalyst also displays excellent methanol tolerance, outstanding stability (90 % current retention after 24 h), and durability (only 18 mV half-wave potential shift after 2000 cyclic voltammetry [CV] cycles). The excellent activity and durability of the catalyst are attributed to the synergistic effect of CuO/ZnO and nitrogen-doped carbon. The structure formation of CuO modified ZnO supported on nitrogen doped carbon (CuO/ZnO/NC-600) catalyst provides the advantages associated with excellent conduction of electrons and a large specific surface area (220 m2/g). These, along with the desirable interfacial charge transfer between CuO and ZnO, aid in obtaining the observed ORR activity in CuO/ZnO/NC-600.

head.ce:title: CuO modified ZnO on nitrogen-doped carbon: a durable and efficient electrocatalyst for oxygen reduction reaction

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The sluggish nature of oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and the current use of expensive precious metals (platinum [Pt], palladium [Pd], etc.) are barriers to the commercialization and scale-up of fuel cell and metal-air battery technologies. Given this, the development of low-cost non-precious metal-based electrocatalysts with high activity and durability is an area that requires attention. In this study, we report cupric oxide (CuO) modified zinc oxide (ZnO) supported on nitrogen-doped carbon (CuO/ZnO/NC-600) as a desirable electrocatalyst given its activity in alkaline medium, durability, and low cost. The CuO/ZnO/NC-600 catalyst shows excellent ORR activity with an onset potential and half-wave potential (E 1/2) of 0.91 V and 0.80 V vs. reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE), respectively, with outstanding limiting current density of 5.34 mA/cm2. The catalyst also displays excellent methanol tolerance, outstanding stability (90 % current retention after 24 h), and durability (only 18 mV half-wave potential shift after 2000 cyclic voltammetry [CV] cycles). The excellent activity and durability of the catalyst are attributed to the synergistic effect of CuO/ZnO and nitrogen-doped carbon. The structure formation of CuO modified ZnO supported on nitrogen doped carbon (CuO/ZnO/NC-600) catalyst provides the advantages associated with excellent conduction of electrons and a large specific surface area (220 m2/g). These, along with the desirable interfacial charge transfer between CuO and ZnO, aid in obtaining the observed ORR activity in CuO/ZnO/NC-600.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental pollution has been increasing at an alarming rate due to the excessive use of fossil fuels. Also, the prevailing shortage of fossil fuels is a major concern for both researchers and governments. There is an urgent need to develop green, sustainable, cost-effective, high-efficiency energy storage, and conversion technologies. Among different energy storage and conversion technologies, fuel cell and metal-air batteries have proven promising [1]. The advantages of fuel cell and metal-air battery energy devices are that they are clean, sustainable, cost-effective, and they also have high efficiency [1–3]. But the major bottleneck associated with these technologies has to do with the sluggish nature of oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in these devices [4].

In fuel cells, ORR is the most important reaction. ORR occurs at the cathode of fuel cells, and hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) occurs at the anode. But ORR is five-to-six times slower than HOR, which hinders cell performance [5]. Hence, to facilitate the key ORR at the cathode, there is a need to use excessive and expensive precious metal catalyst (Pt/C), which is the main challenge associated with the commercialization of these technologies [1,5].

Till now, platinum (Pt) and Pt alloy-based catalysts have been studied extensively for ORR [6,7]. Nonetheless, Pt/C still remains the benchmark electrocatalyst for ORR [4]. However, major issues with this benchmark electrocatalyst are associated with its lack of stability, fuel crossover, high price (up to 50 % of device cost), and scarcity [1,5,6,8]. These are the factors that hinder these technologies from becoming commercialized. Hence it is a priority to develop platinum group metals (PGMs, such as platinum [Pt], palladium [Pd], rhodium [Rh], ruthenium [Ru], iridium [Ir], and osmium [Os])-free ORR catalysts with excellent activity and stability. To address these issues, different classes of materials are being explored [9–17].

Among fuel cells, polymer electrolyte membrane fuel cells (PEMFCs) and anion exchange membrane fuel cells (AEMFCs) have recently gained attention as clean energy devices as they have high efficiency (60%), lower operating temperature, and zero pollution [1–3]. However, PEMFCs mainly depend on expensive Pt-based electrocatalysts, which are prohibitive in costs. Hence currently, as a replacement for PEMFCs, AEMFCs have gained attention [18,19]. In AEMFCs, the use of non-precious metal electrocatalysts, which are stable in alkaline media become possible [20,21].

The advantages of AEMFC catalysts are that they tend to have high corrosion resistance in alkaline medium, less fuel-crossover effect, and high efficiency, and they cost much less than Pt-group metals such as Pt, Pd, Rh, Ru, Ir, and Os [20,21]. As far as ORR catalysts are concerned, metal nitrides [14,22], metal carbides [14], metal oxides [16,23–27], metal oxynitrides [12,13], and metal-free catalysts [15,28,29] have been reported. Among the different catalysts, transition metal oxides are gaining attention because of their excellent redox behavior. This is due to the various oxidation states and higher stability associated with these materials [23,24]. Also, these catalysts are inexpensive and can be produced in scale.

In the context of this work, notable results on transition metal oxides-based ORR are the following: Huang et al. have reported tricobalt tetroxide (Co3O4) nanoparticles anchored on nitrogen-doped partially exfoliated multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) for enhanced ORR activity [30]. Maiti et al. have studied ferric oxide–molybdenum trioxide (Fe2O3–MoO3)-based binary oxides supported on nitrogen-doped graphene for ORR with a half-wave potential of 0.82 V vs. reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) [16]. Liang et al. have fabricated Co3O4 nanoparticles on nitrogen-doped reduced graphene oxide (N-rGO), which shows excellent ORR activity because of the excellent synergistic effect between Co3O4 and N-rGO [4]. Goswami et al. also observed tricobalt tetroxide-cerium dioxide (Co3O4/CeO2)-based binary oxides supported with carbon for ORR [31]. We too have recently reported an efficient ORR catalyst based on a heterostructure between doped graphene quantum dot and titanium dioxide (TiO2). The half-wave potential (E 1/2) of the catalyst is 0.82 V vs. RHE [23].

Different metal oxides such as TiO2, zinc oxide (ZnO), cerium dioxide (CeO2), etc., are known for their outstanding chemical stability. Also, they have better corrosion resistance [32–34]. Zinc oxide, a transition metal oxide, is a group II-IV semiconductor material prone to oxygen vacancy formation [35–37]. It has been a topic of research due to its remarkable optoelectronic, photocatalytic properties, and chemical sensing properties [38–46]. Furthermore, it is environmentally friendly, chemically stable, inexpensive, and, more importantly, has high electron mobility [47]. It may be noted that ZnO has been rarely explored as an electrocatalyst for ORR. Yu et al. reported ZnO oxides nanoparticles dispersed on reduced graphene oxides (rGO) for ORR [48]. They have claimed improved ORR performance due to a synergistic effect between ZnO and rGO. Also, Sun et al. have reported Co-doped ZnO spread in reduced graphene oxides for ORR [25]. Based on their observation, the improved ORR performance is due to the synergistic effect of Co2+ and Zn2+. Also, Shakil et al. have examined the different dopants on ZnO for ORR [49].

However, the main problem associated with ZnO or for that purpose, metal oxides in general, is their intrinsic insulating behavior [50,51]. Hence, developing using metal oxide heterostructures and nanocarbon support can be an effective way of improving ORR activity [16,17,24,31,52–56]. Nanocarbons change the charge distribution and charge transport that occur at the interface [52–56]. Also, instead of monometallic oxides, the use of binary oxides helps to get multiple active sites and charge redistribution at the interface [16,17,24,31]. Nano-structuring and modification of interfaces are considered effective tactics for the advancement of active ORR catalysts [24,26,27,30,50].

From the standpoint of making a heterostructure with ZnO, cupric oxide (CuO) is particularly attractive. CuO is an intrinsic p-type semiconductor with a narrow bandgap (1.2–1.9 eV) [47]. It is also clear from the literature that the junction formed between ZnO and CuO is good for charge transfer applications such as photocatalyst and sensing applications [43,44,47,51,57–59]. Given that ORR too depends on charge transfer, it is reasonable to explore heterojunctions of ZnO and CuO as ORR electrocatalysts.

In this study, we have seen a desirable synergistic effect between CuO and ZnO for enhancing ORR. Here we have synthesized CuO/ZnO-based heterojunctions, and then this heterostructure is supported with nitrogen-doped carbon heated at 600°C in an argon (Ar) atmosphere for 2h. The presence of oxygen vacancies, too, must add to the synergistic effect. Also, we have seen that ZnO's stability aids in the protection of the activity of CuO, thereby giving good stability and durability to the composite compared to individual CuO and nitrogen-doped carbon.

Zinc acetate dihydrate (SRL), copper acetate monohydrate (Sigma Aldrich), 20 % Pt/C (Sigma Aldrich), melamine (Merck), Ketjenblack carbon (KBC) (EC-600JD) (C) (Nouryon), and Nafion (5 wt.%) (Sigma Aldrich), are procured for this study.

Initially, 2 g of C is dispersed into a concentrated nitric acid [HNO3] (100 mL) solution and heated at 70 °C for 6 h. After that, the solution is washed using de-ionized water (DI) repeatedly to neutralize the solution, and the solution is dried at 60 °C for 12 h. Then the dried C powder (0.7 g) and melamine (0.35 g) are adequately mixed with grinding, and then the ground mixture is heated at 700 °C in an N2 atmosphere for 2 h to get N-doped carbon (NC).

First of all, cupric oxide (CuO)/zinc oxide (ZnO)-based heterostructure is synthesized using the previously described hydrothermal method with marginal modification [60]. Copper acetate monohydrate and zinc acetate dihydrate in 1: 4 M ratios are dispersed in 20 mL de-ionized (DI) water. After that, sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solution (3 M) is added to the solution to get pH = 12. Then the obtained solution is transferred to an autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 9 h. After that, the product is washed with DI water and ethanol repeatedly and dried at 70 °C overnight. Then the dried powder is heated at 500 °C for 2 h in a muffle furnace with a heating rate of 2 °C/min. CuO nanoparticles and ZnO nanorods are separately prepared using the same method without adding zinc for CuO preparation and copper precursor for ZnO preparation, respectively.

To make CuO/ZnO/NC-600 nanocomposite, the synthesized CuO/ZnO and NC are taken in a 1:1 ratio and, after grinding, are kept in a tubular furnace at 600 °C in an argon (Ar) atmosphere for 2 h. CuO/NC-600 and ZnO/NC-600 are also synthesized with the same procedure (Scheme 1 ).

In the preparation, 6 mg catalyst material is first dispersed properly in 500 μL of water and ethanol mixture (1:1). Then 30 μL Nafion solution (5 wt.%) is used as a binder, and ultrasonication is done for proper dispersion. After that, the prepared catalyst ink is used for the study. The catalyst loading for all the experiments is 0.48 mg /cm2.

X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku and Supermini Flex 6G) with Cu Kα (λ = 1.5406 Å) at 40 KV and 15 mA is used for X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement. Rietveld refinement of the XRD pattern is performed using FullProf Software suite®. A space group of P 63 m c for ZnO and C 1 2/c 1 for CuO are used, and the structural parameters are refined. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) (200 V-30 kV) and energy dispersive X-ray (EDAX) are used for morphology and elemental analysis of the samples. Tecnai G2 T20 is used to obtain transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and selected area electron diffraction pattern (SAED) images. High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images are collected from JEOL 3010. Raman spectra are collected from HORIBA JOBIN VYON HR UV 800 with 488 nm excitation. JASCO-4100 Type A is used to collect Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectrum. Omicron ESCA Probe X-ray photoelectron spectrometer is used for X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements. Al Kα X-ray is used for the XPS measurements. Specific surface area is measured using Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) setup (JWGB Micro 122W). Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) measurement is performed at room temperature on a JEOL JES F200 X-band spectrometer operating at a frequency of 9.42 GHz with field modulation frequency of 100 kHz. The Magic Angle Spinning (MAS) NMR experiments were performed on a 400 MHz JEOL spectrometer at 10 kHz spinning frequency using a 4 mm H-X probe. Direct 13C spectra were recorded using a 20 s recycle delay and pulse length of 3.5 μs in each case. The spectra are referenced to room temperature adamantane 13C peaks at 37.8 and 28.5 ppm.

A three-electrode pyrex glass system is used to do all the electrochemical characterizations. Catalyst-coated glassy carbon electrode (GCE), Pt wire, and potassium chloride (KCl) saturated Ag/AgCl is used as the working, counter, and reference electrode, respectively. Metrohm multi-Autolab M204 (MAC90009) potentiostat is used for cyclic voltammetry (CV) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) experiment. Rotating ring disk electrode (RRDE) (Pine Instrument) is used to evaluate ORR kinetics. The RRDE experiments are performed on a 5.5 mm diameter GCE disk surrounded by a Pt ring (with an outward diameter 8.5 mm and inward diameter of 6.5 mm). 0.1 M KOH solution is used as an electrolyte. All the potentials are transformed to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) via Eqs. (1) and (2),

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is carried out in the frequency range of 100 kHz to 100 Hz with 5 mV applied AC voltage. The EIS data is fitted using ZSimpWin software.

The composition and purity of the samples are determined using X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. Fig. 1 a shows XRD patterns of ZnO, CuO/ZnO, nanocomposite composed of NC-supported CuO modified ZnO catalyst (CuO/ZnO/NC), and CuO/ZnO/NC-600. It shows XRD peaks at 31.70°, 34.36°, 36.19°, 47.48°, 56.54°, 62.81°, 66.33°, 67.89°, 69.02°, corresponding to the ZnO with hexagonal wurtzite structure (Joint Committee on Powder Diffraction Standards; now known as: ICDD: International Centre for Diffraction Data [JCPDS] No. 36–1451). The sharp peaks reveal high crystallinity [42]. In addition, a few minor peaks have appeared at 35.54°, 38.76°, 48.6°, 53.1°, and 61.5°, which are the diffraction peaks from CuO nanoparticles with the monoclinic structure (JCPDS No. 80–0076). We have found a minor peak at around ∼42.5° is plausibly a Cu2O/Cu peak.

Fig. 1b shows a zoomed version of the XRD plot at a particular region for a better understanding of CuO-related peaks. Also, in the case of CuO/ZnO/NC and CuO/ZnO/NC-600 samples, the inset of Fig. 1b shows the presence of nitrogen-doped carbon (NC) with a broad peak at 24°. Fig. 1c shows the peak shifts separately for (100) and (002) planes of the ZnO, CuO/ZnO, CuO/ZnO/NC, and CuO/ZnO/NC-600 samples (Inset shows XRD pattern of NC). The XRD peaks of pure zinc oxide (ZnO) nanorod are shifted to higher angles in the case of the CuO/ZnO sample. The shift is possibly due to incorporating a small fraction of Cu in ZnO [46,61]. The shift is attributable to the smaller ionic radius of Cu2+ (0.73 Å) compared to the ionic radius of Zn2+ (0.74 Å) [61]. The lattice parameters are calculated and listed in Table 1 . The crystallite size (D) is also measured for all the samples from the familiar Scherrer's formula [23] and listed in Table 1. It is also observed that after adding NC, the peak shift occurs towards smaller angles compared to pure ZnO and nanocomposite CuO/ZnO in CuO/ZnO/NC and CuO/ZnO/NC-600 samples. This indicates lattice parameter expansion due to the creation of tensile strain in the CuO/ZnO/NC and CuO/ZnO/NC-600 samples [23,60,62]. The details of lattice parameters and unit cell volume are listed for all the samples in Table 1. The lattice expansion for the CuO/ZnO/NC and CuO/ZnO/NC-600 samples could be attributed to the addition of NC. It creates lattice distortion in the CuO/ZnO lattice resulting in lattice expansion. The CuO/ZnO/NC-600 sample shows the highest lattice distortion. This geometric effect (tensile strain) also helps to get enhanced oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) activity [9,10,63]. Also, for better understanding, we have compared XRD data separately for CuO and CuO/ZnO/NC-600 samples in Fig. 1d and Fig. 1e. Rietveld refinement for CuO/ZnO/NC-600 samples has been done to know the volume fraction of CuO and ZnO in the nanocomposites. The corresponding plot is shown in Fig. S1. We find 83.5 vol% ZnO phase and 16.5 vol% CuO. The related parameters are listed in Table S1.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum has been taken to observe different functional groups, in particular, the metal-oxygen bonds in the synthesized samples. Fig. 2 a shows the FTIR spectrum of ZnO, CuO/ZnO, CuO/ZnO/NC, and CuO/ZnO/NC-600 samples in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. The peak at ∼3427 cm−1 shows OH stretching in all the samples. The peaks at around 1400–1600 cm−1 in ZnO, CuO/ZnO, CuO/ZnO/NC, and CuO/ZnO/NC-600 show the presence of metal carbonyl groups (C=O at ∼1600 cm−1, and C–O at 1190 cm−1). In addition, an absorption signal corresponding to C=C appears at 1350 cm−1. The broad absorption peak in the range of 400–600 cm−1 indicates metal-oxygen bonds. The peak at 429 cm−1 shows Zn–O bond formation. Also, we notice a peak at 483 cm−1, which shows overlapped Cu–O bond formation and oxygen vacancy-related peaks of Zn–O [64,65]. These two peaks seem to be merging. For better understanding, we have plotted FTIR spectrum of CuO alone in Fig. S2a. Also, inset of Fig. S2a. shows comparative FTIR spectrum of CuO, ZnO, and CuO/ZnO/NC-600. It shows the presence of Cu–O and Zn–O absorption peaks in the CuO/ZnO/NC-600 nanocomposite.

First of all, we note that some major XRD peaks related to CuO are in fact absent in this case. Given this backdrop, and considering that the FTIR peak at ∼485 cm−1 is somewhat broad (inset of Fig. S2a), it is likely that there are copper-rich phases as well. This is reasonable, given that there could be incomplete reaction and also because copper formation can be promoted due to presence in carbon in the annealing conditions chosen. Hence a mix of Cu-rich oxide phases, and plausibly even Cu itself, is present in the nanocomposite.

Fig. 2b shows the room temperature Raman spectrum of ZnO, CuO/ZnO, CuO/ZnO/NC, and CuO/ZnO/NC-600 samples. This Raman spectrum also confirms the successful formation of nanocomposite CuO/ZnO-based heterojunction. The Raman spectra of pure ZnO further plotted in Fig. S2b for better understanding. The Raman spectrum of pure ZnO shows typical Raman peaks at 97 cm−1, 329 cm−1, 379 cm−1, 436 cm−1, 580 cm−1, and 1130 cm−1. The peak centered at 97 cm−1 is called E2 low Raman mode, corresponding to the Zn sublattice. The peak at 436 cm−1 is called E2 high Raman mode corresponding to the oxygen motion in the lattice. These two peaks are direct measures of ZnO crystal quality [37,66]. The peak at 329 cm−1 corresponds to E2 high- E2 low mode. The peak detected at 379 cm−1 corresponds to the vibrational AI transverse optical mode. The peak at 580 cm−1 corresponds to AIL mode, which is denoted as the oxygen vacancy peak in the ZnO structure [67–69]. The Raman spectrum shows the presence of an oxygen vacancy peak in all the mentioned catalysts.

Along with these peaks, two additional peaks at 286 cm−1 and 627 cm−1 are also found in CuO attachment samples corresponding to the A1g and B2g modes of CuO due to vibration of oxygen atoms [47,70]. For better understanding, we have plotted the Raman spectrum of CuO alone in Fig. S2c. E2 high Raman mode attributes the oxygen sublattice and signifies the wurtzite phase of ZnO, which is used to define the crystal quality of ZnO. Due to the introduction of defects (oxygen vacancy), the crystal quality of ZnO is reduced. Table 2 shows comparison data of the full width at half maximum (FWHM) and peak shifting of E2 high peak for different samples. We observe a gradual decrease of the mode and a redshift (Fig. 2c) of the E2 high peak. It could be due to the increase in defect concentration (oxygen vacancy) [37,66]. The creation of the oxygen vacancies could reduce the average atomic mass. It causes mass variations at the oxygen positions, which result in a redshift [67–69]. The oxygen vacancies are highest in the heated sample CuO/ZnO/NC-600, reflecting the highest ORR activity [71,72]. Also, it can be observed that compared to pure ZnO for other samples, the Raman bands are broader and less symmetric (a gradual increase of peak broadening), which is most likely caused by the formation of oxygen vacancy [36,67–69]. The broadening indicates of reduction in crystallinity due to introducing defects.

The CuO/ZnO/NC and CuO/ZnO/NC-600 samples also show the presence of D and G peaks corresponding to carbon after the addition of NC (Fig. 2b). Also, Fig. 2d shows the Raman spectrum of NC. The defective nature of a carbon material is measured by the ratio of ID/IG. For good quality of carbon material, the ID/IG value would be smaller than 1 [15,16]. G peaks correspond to E2g mode of sp2 carbon, and the D peak indicates the disorder of the sp2 domain. We calculated the ID/IG value of the NC (ID/IG = 1.34), CuO/ZnO/NC (ID/IG = 1.45) and CuO/ZnO/NC-600 (ID/IG = 1.58) samples. It can be seen that the ID/IG is increased in NC after the addition of CuO/ZnO. It shows electronic interaction between NC and CuO/ZnO heterostructure. This points to the successful decoration of CuO/ZnO samples on NC. Also, higher ID/IG implies more disorder associated with NC in CuO/ZnO/NC-600. This indicates the surface defects plausibly act catalytically active sites [15,16,23,28,73,74].

Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) measurement is conducted at room temperature, as shown in Fig. S3. The signal associated with singly ionized oxygen vacancy (Vo) is due to unpaired electrons trapped at oxygen vacancies [67,75,76]. We have observed a low field signal with a g factor ∼1.997, which is attributed to the unpaired electron trapped on an oxygen vacancy site [77–79]. It indicates the presence of oxygen vacancy in the materials. It can be concluded that the sample with higher signal intensity is associated with a higher oxygen vacancy concentration [67,75–79]. We observe the highest intensity for the CuO/ZnO/NC-600 sample, indicating higher oxygen vacancy concentration than the CuO/ZnO and CuO/ZnO/NC samples, which is consistent with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Raman results.

In the 13C solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) data Ketjenblack carbon [KBC (C)] sample, peaks corresponding to the epoxy and hydroxyl groups are observed below 75 ppm, as shown in Fig. S4 [80,81]. These peaks are absent in the NC sample, probably due to the reductive environment offered by melamine during heat treatment. The KBC (C) sample also shows a broad peak spanning the 75–175 ppm range corresponding to sp2 carbons [80,81]. The NC and CuO/ZnO/NC-600 samples show a shoulder peak at around 150 ppm due to the presence of N. N 1s high-resolution XPS (Fig. 6f) scan confirms the presence of pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N in the CuO/ZnO/NC-600 sample. The oxygen functional groups in the region below 75 ppm are restored in CuO/ZnO/NC-600 sample, with a little shift in the ppm. These oxygen functional groups may be from the surface of oxide particles. Thus, the 13C solid-state NMR and XPS data combined with the observation that the enhanced ORR activity of CuO/ZnO/NC-600 could be attributed to the surface oxygen functional groups of metal oxides and N-doped sites on the carbon, which together catalyze the ORR.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) is used to analyze the morphology of the samples. Fig. 3 a and b show the SEM images of the CuO/ZnO sample at different magnifications. From the images, it can be seen that CuO nanoparticles are decorated on top of the ZnO nanorod. Fig. 3c shows SEM image of CuO/ZnO/NC-600 sample. This image shows that the CuO/ZnO system is dispersed on NC. We have marked different regions for better understanding. Fig. 3d shows the size distribution of CuO nanoparticles with an average size is 74.2 nm. The ZnO nanorods have a size in the micrometer range with a length ∼1.3 μm and a diameter of ∼0.6 μm. Also, we have provided an SEM image of NC alone (Fig. S5a). An additional SEM image of CuO/ZnO/NC-600 sample is shown in Fig. S5b. We have also marked different regions for better understanding.

Elemental analysis has been done using EDAX attached with SEM to investigate the purity of the material. Fig. 4 a shows SEM image using which elemental analysis is done. Fig. 4b shows EDAX spectrum of CuO/ZnO/NC-600 composite. Fig. 4c–h shows the elemental mapping of CuO/ZnO/NC-600 composite. The presence of all the elements (copper [Cu], zinc [Zn], nitrogen [N], carbon [C], and oxygen [O]) indicates the successful formation of the composite. The presence of N also confirmed the successful formation of nitrogen-doped carbon. EDAX mapping images also show the relatively uniform distribution of all the elements in the composites. We have also provided additional EDAX mapping (Figs. S5c–h) of the CuO/ZnO/NC-600 sample, where we observe the presence of Cu, Zn, O, N and C in a better way.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs have been taken for the synthesized samples to gain more information about the morphology. Fig. 5 a shows TEM image of CuO/ZnO material, showing ZnO nanorods are successfully decorated with CuO nanoparticles. The size of CuO nanoparticles is 60–80 nm. Fig. 5b shows the TEM image of the CuO/ZnO/NC-600 sample. It shows that the CuO/ZnO system is further dispersed on NC. We have shown an additional TEM image of CuO/ZnO/NC-600 in Fig. S5i, where we see the decoration of CuO/ZnO on NC. Fig. 5c shows HR-TEM image of the CuO/ZnO/NC-600, which allows the observation of the material's lattice spacing. The lattice spacing is observed to be 0.281 nm, 0.265 nm, and 0.260 nm corresponding to (100), (002) planes of ZnO, and (111) planes of CuO [44,82,83]. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern is shown in Fig. 5d for the CuO/ZnO/NC-600 sample. The SAED offers two kinds of diffraction spots. The bright and clear diffraction patterns come from a single crystalline ZnO nanorod. The concentric ring pattern can be indexed with the polycrystalline CuO [82,83]. All these results indicate the successful formation of CuO/ZnO-based heterostructure.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) has been done to investigate the surface elemental composition and oxidation state of the elements. The survey spectrum of CuO/ZnO sample is also shown in Fig. S6a. The survey spectrum of CuO/ZnO/NC-600 sample is shown in Fig. S6b. Fig. S6b displays the existence of all the elements, zinc (Zn 2p3/2) (1024.35 eV), Zn 2p1/2 (1047.33 eV), copper (Cu 2p3/2) (935.43 eV), Cu 2p1/2 (953.488 eV), oxygen (O 1s) (532.96eV), nitrogen (N 1s) (398.084 eV), and carbon (C 1s) (284.815 eV). Fig. 6 a–c show high-resolution XPS spectra of O1s for the samples of CuO/ZnO, CuO/ZnO/NC, and CuO/ZnO/NC-600. It has been seen that three deconvoluted peaks in O1s correspond to lattice oxygen (530.1 eV), oxygen vacancy (531.27 eV) [84–88], and adsorbed oxygen (532.998 eV). It is clear that among the three samples, the area under the curve corresponding to the oxygen vacancy peak is more for CuO/ZnO/NC-600 sample. This is followed by areas under the curve in the case of CuO/ZnO/NC and CuO/ZnO. It implies that oxygen vacancy concentration is more in the CuO/ZnO/NC-600 sample. Furthermore, for further analysis, the area ratio of oxygen vacancy peak to the total area of O1s peak is evaluated for all samples. It is observed that the peak area ratio related to the oxygen vacancy peak is more in the CuO/ZnO/NC-600 compared to the CuO/ZnO/NC and CuO/ZnO. Results do point to the possibility that higher vacancy concentration in CuO/ZnO/NC-600 aids in obtaining better ORR activity.

Fig. 6d shows the high-resolution XPS of Zn 2p. The deconvoluted peaks are at ∼1021.518 eV (Zn 2p3/2) and∼1044.58 eV (Zn 2p1/2). Fig. 6e shows high-resolution XPS of Cu 2p. Four peaks are used to deconvolute high-resolution XPS spectra of Cu 2p. The presence of Cu 2p3/2 (932.81) and Cu 2p1/2 (952.8 eV) is inferred. Also, two satellite peaks are observed at ∼941.9 eV and ∼962.2 eV. It indicates the existence of CuO in the composite.

The high-resolution XPS spectra of N1s is shown in Fig. 6f. It shows the presence of three kinds of N atoms which are pyridinic N (397.82 eV), pyrrolic N (399.11 eV), and graphite N (400.53 eV). The ORR activity depends on the different configurations of N atoms. It is known that limiting current density is influenced by graphitic N configuration along with onset potential for ORR is influenced by pyridinic N configuration [89,90]. Fig. S6c shows high-resolution XPS of C 1s. It is fitted using three peaks at 283.6 eV (C = C), 284.7 eV (C–C) and 287.4 eV (C = O).

N2 adsorption/desorption analysis is performed at −196 °C to determine the catalysts' porous nature and specific surface area. All the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms (Fig. 7 ) show characteristics associated with type IV isotherms. An H3-type hysteresis loop is also observed in the CuO/ZnO/NC and CuO/ZnO/NC-600 samples in a relatively high-pressure region. This indicates the mesoporous nature of the catalysts.

The inset of Fig. 7a–c shows the corresponding Barrett−Joyner−Halenda (BJH) pore size distribution curves. All the catalysts have a major pore diameter of ∼3.5 nm, indicating their mesoporous structure. The Brunauer−Emmett−Teller (BET) multipoint method is used to determine the specific surface area of the catalysts. The specific surface area of the catalysts is 220 m2/g (CuO/ZnO/NC-600), 140 m2/g (CuO/ZnO/NC), and 4 m2/g (CuO/ZnO). The highest surface area is observed for CuO/ZnO/NC-600 sample. This too, could be a contributing factor to the better ORR performance seen in CuO/ZnO/NC-600 compared to the CuO/ZnO and CuO/ZnO/NC samples.

The electrochemical ORR performance of the synthesized catalysts has been evaluated with a three-electrode setup. Fig. 8 a shows cyclic voltammetries (CVs) in both N2 and O2 saturated conditions for CuO/ZnO/NC-600 catalyst in 0.1 M KOH solution. In O2 saturated condition, CuO/ZnO/NC-600 shows two cathodic peaks at 0.79 V vs. reversible hydrogen electrode [RHE] (peak potential), the other one is at 0.53 V vs. RHE (peak potential), and one anodic peak at 0.93 V vs. RHE (peak potential). In the N2 saturated condition for CuO/ZnO/NC-600, the cathodic peak at 0.79 V vs. RHE does not appear, indicating the peak is due to the ORR in the catalysts. The other cathodic peak and anodic peak are linked to a redox reaction of copper [91–93].

The anodic peak at 0.93 V vs. RHE is attributed to CuO film formation. Alongside, a cathodic peak is observed at 0.53 V. It could be due to the partial reduction of cupric oxide (CuO) to cuprous oxide (Cu2O) [91–93]. Fig. 8b compares the CV behavior of CuO/ZnO/NC-600 with that of CuO/ZnO, CuO/ZnO/NC, NC, NC-600, and 20% Pt/C catalysts. The O2 reduction peak potential is observed at 0.79 V vs. RHE for CuO/ZnO/NC-600, whereas CuO/ZnO, CuO/ZnO/NC, NC, NC-600, and Pt/C show the potential at 0.55, 0.76, 0.71, 0.72 and 0.83 V vs. RHE. The cathodic peak current density is observed around 2.42 mA/cm2 for CuO/ZnO/NC-600, whereas CuO/ZnO, CuO/ZnO/NC, NC, NC-600, and Pt/C show 0.36, 1.51, 1.36, 2.1 and 1.29 mA/cm2. These results reflect that the catalyst CuO/ZnO/NC-600 shows excellent ORR activity.

The ORR activity for the different control samples have been compared at 1600 rpm in Fig. 8c. The onset potential for CuO/ZnO/NC-600 catalyst is 0.91 V vs. RHE, which is better compared to CuO/ZnO (0.65 V), NC (0.83 V), NC-600 (0.84) and CuO/ZnO/NC (0.88 V). The half-wave potential (E 1/2) of the CuO/ZnO/NC-600 is 0.80 V vs. RHE, which is better when compared to the CuO/ZnO (0.566 V), NC (0.71 V), NC-600 (0.73 V) and CuO/ZnO/NC (0.77 V) catalysts. The limiting current density for CuO/ZnO, NC, NC-600, and CuO/ZnO/NC catalysts are observed around 0.81 mA/cm2, 3.85 mA/cm2, 4.7 mA/cm2, and 4.17 mA/cm2, whereas CuO/ZnO/NC-600 catalyst, the limiting current density is 5.34 mA/cm2. The limiting current density of standard Pt/C catalyst is 5 mA/cm2. Fig. 8d shows the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) graph of CuO/ZnO/NC-600 at different rotations. It indicates that the ORR activity is diffusion-limited.

These results reflect that our optimized catalyst CuO/ZnO/NC-600 is comparable with standard Pt/C in onset potential and half-wave potential. CuO/ZnO/NC-600 outperformed Pt/C in terms of limiting current density. This excellent ORR activity is attributed to the excellent synergistic effect between CuO/ZnO and NC. It can be seen that CuO/ZnO/NC-600 shows the highest specific surface area with mesoporous nature (Fig. 7c) compared to CuO/ZnO and CuO/ZnO/NC. It helps to get excellent ORR activity in CuO/ZnO/NC-600 sample. Also, the CuO/ZnO based heterostructure helps in obtaining enhanced ORR activity with the aid of fast charge transfer through the interface of CuO and ZnO. The calcination temperature for the catalyst has also been optimized based on the ORR performance (Fig. S7a).

The control sample analysis has also been done to find out the original reason for the ORR activity. Fig. S7b shows the LSV data comparison between CuO/ZnO/NC-600, CuO/NC-600, and ZnO/NC-600. Here, we also observe the excellent improvement of limiting current density as well as enhancement in half-wave and onset potential in the CuO/ZnO/NC-600 sample compared to the CuO/NC-600 and ZnO/NC-600 samples. It shows that CuO/ZnO system is performing better when compared to individual CuO and ZnO, respectively. Further, because of an abundance of oxygen vacancies in ZnO, ORR activity enhancement occurs in the CuO/ZnO/NC-600 sample (Fig. 6c).

Also, the observed performance enhancement could be attributed to the synergistic effect of Cu2+ ions and Zn2+ ions for enhanced ORR. The CuO nanoparticles decorated over the ZnO surface and the strong coupling between CuO/ZnO and conductive NC offer sufficient surface area (Fig. 7c) to adsorb oxygen and facilitate the fast electron transport during the ORR process. The porous structure of the CuO/ZnO/NC-600 composite, shown in N2 adsorption-desorption (Fig. 7c), can provide more accessible active sites to enhance the ORR activity. Also, from Fig. S7c, one can see the advantages of NC compared to undoped carbon. The NC offers a better synergistic effect with CuO/ZnO compared to carbon for ORR. The comparison of the reported catalyst with previous similar (CuO and ZnO based) reports is made in Table 3 . We observe that only doping or using nanocarbon support alone is not enough to get enhanced ORR activity out of ZnO system. We see that simultaneously doping on ZnO and using nanocarbon as support helps to improve ORR activity. We have observed similar trends for CuO-based systems, although the intrinsically catalytic activity of the CuO system is better than ZnO. It is also observed that both CuO and ZnO have stability issues when they are used individually as catalysts. However, when used together, as in the reported hybrid system (CuO/ZnO/NC-600), we observed much better ORR activity and stability.

The K–L analysis (Fig. 9 a) is done to find out the number of electrons (n) involved in the ORR. To calculate the n, Eqs. (3)–(6) are used.

It is known that ORR happens via either a four-electron (4e−) process or a two-electron (2e−) ORR process. In the 4e− process, O2 is directly reduced to H2O, unlike 2e− process (formation of hydrogen peroxide [H2O2] intermediate). Usually, a 4e− process is considered ideal one for faster ORR. CuO/ZnO/NC-600 catalyst shows a high electron transfer number (n = 3.85), indicating an approximate 4e− ORR process on it.

Fig. 9b shows the Tafel plots using Eq. (4) for the catalysts. The Tafel slope for the CuO/ZnO/NC-600 catalyst is 68 mV/dec, showing significantly better kinetics compared to CuO/ZnO (71 mV/dec) and CuO/ZnO/NC (77 mV/dec) catalysts. This higher catalytic activity can be attributed to a synergistic effect between CuO/ZnO and carbon support. Furthermore, heat treatment at 600 °C helps with obtaining optimized results from the composites.

Standard accelerated durability test (ADT) is conducted to investigate the durability of the CuO/ZnO/NC-600 catalyst in the potential window of 0.1 and 1.1 V vs. RHE in a 0.1 M KOH aqueous solution. Fig. 10 a shows the comparison LSV plot of CuO/ZnO/NC-600 catalyst initial and after 2000 cycles. Only an18 mV shift that occurs in half-wave potential and there is no change in onset potential for CuO/ZnO/NC-600 catalyst, even after 2000 cycles of CVs. It indicates excellent cycling stability of the CuO/ZnO/NC-600 catalyst.

The ADT test is also conducted (Fig. S7d) for the CuO/NC-600 sample to examine zinc oxide's role in our studies. The CuO/NC-600 sample shows significantly less cycling stability (ΔE 1/2 = 45 mV and ΔE onset = 15 mV) compare to CuO/ZnO/NC-600 catalyst. It indicates that CuO/ZnO/NC-600 catalyst is superior in terms of activity as well as durability compare to an individual constituent one. Here also it can be confirmed that ZnO is responsible for providing better durability in the final catalyst. Methanol tolerance is an important parameter to evaluate the practical utility for fuel cell application. For the CuO/ZnO/NC-600 catalyst, the methanol tolerance is investigated in 1 M aqueous methanol solution. Fig. 10b shows that the LSV is performed at CuO/ZnO/NC-600 catalysts with high methanol tolerance, unlike Pt/C. The Pt/C shows a prominent methanol oxidation peak.

A chronoamperometric test is conducted to examine the stability of the CuO/ZnO/NC-600 catalysts for 24 h at 0.55 V at 800 rpm. Fig. 10c shows excellent stability with the current retention of 90 % for CuO/ZnO/NC-600, which is better than the standard Pt/C catalyst. The improved stability and durability of the CuO/ZnO/NC-600 catalyst can be attributed to the synergistic presence of CuO, ZnO, and the carbon support, which brings about an electronic modification in the resultant structure with improved activity.

In short, the origin of enhanced ORR activity and stability of CuO/ZnO/NC-600 could be understood based on the following points: (1) The strong coupling between CuO/ZnO and NC helps effectively to increase the electron transfer ability and provides active sites for ORR; (2) sufficient number of oxygen vacancy presence in CuO/ZnO helps to improve electrical conductivity, which helps provide a more catalytic active site for ORR; (3) the N doping into [KBC (C)] creates uneven charge distribution, which is helpful for easy adsorbing of oxygen molecules; (iv) besides, desirable charge transfer across the CuO–ZnO interface could be helpful for ORR activity. All above-mentioned results suggest the strongly coupled CuO/ZnO/NC-600 catalyst is promising for ORR application.

A novel nanocomposite composed of NC-supported CuO modified ZnO is synthesized using a simple and facile hydrothermal method. XRD is used to confirm the ZnO phase (wurtzite crystal structure) and CuO phase (monoclinic crystal structure) formation of the catalyst. FTIR, Raman, HR-TEM, and XPS are also used for confirmation of the nanocomposite. Electrochemical testing is performed to evaluate the ORR. The CuO/ZnO/NC-600 catalyst shows excellent ORR activity with an onset potential and E 1/2 of 0.91 V and 0.80 V vs. RHE, respectively, compared with the benchmark Pt/C. The CuO/ZnO/NC-600 catalysts outperformed benchmark Pt/C with an outstanding limiting current density (5.34 mA/cm2). The Tafel slope 68 mV/dec indicates excellent kinetics of the catalyst. The catalyst shows excellent methanol tolerance, outstanding stability (90 % current retention after 24 h), and durability (only 18 mV E 1/2 shift after 2000 CV cycles). The catalyst shows an almost 4 e− (3.85) transfer during ORR and the H2O2 production is close to 5 %. The enhanced ORR performance could be attributed to the synergetic effects of CuO/ZnO and NC in CuO/ZnO/NC-600 composite. The structure formation (CuO/ZnO/NC-600) provides the advantages associated with excellent conduction of electrons and a large specific surface area (220 m2/g). These, along with the desirable interfacial charge transfer between CuO and ZnO, aids in obtaining the observed ORR activity in CuO/ZnO/NC-600.
